purpose: Defines the !env_check slash command — read-only audit verifying that every environment variable referenced in code exists in corresponding .env files, and that config keys are consistent across client/server contexts. Greps for process.env.X (Node), import.meta.env.X (Vite), NEXT_PUBLIC_X (Next.js), Deno.env.get, os.environ/os.getenv (Python), defineString/defineInt (Firebase Functions), and ${VAR} substitution. Locates all .env, .env.local, .env.development, .env.production, .env.example, .env.template, firebase.json files. Cross-references and reports: missing variables, missing-from-template variables, undocumented variables (in .env but not .env.example), and cross-context naming inconsistencies (e.g., NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL vs API_BASE_URL).
gotchas: NEVER output env var VALUES — only key names, existence, and consistency. Outputting values would leak secrets.
Never modify .env files — report only. The user manages secrets.
Cross-context naming drift (client uses NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL, server uses API_BASE_URL) must be flagged as warning even when both technically exist — they may hold mismatched values.
Undocumented vars (present in .env but not in .env.example) are documentation gaps — surface them.
patterns: Multi-language env var pattern detection: Node/Vite/Next.js/Deno/Python/Firebase all checked.
Three-axis check: code references → .env files → .env.example completeness.
